data.title: Nicki Minaj says she's probably Trump's 'number one fan'
data.description:
Nicki Minaj is calling herself President Trump’s biggest fan, saying her support for him won’t waiver despite the “hate” from critics. 

“I will say that I am probably the president’s number one fan,” the “Starships” rapper told the crowd Wednesday at a Treasury Department-hosted summit in Washington to launch “Trump Accounts” for newborn babies.

“And that’s not going to change,” the 43-year-old performer continued.

“The hate or what people have to say, it does not affect me at all. It actually motivates me to support him more, and it’s going to motivate all of us to support him more,” Minaj, who was born Onika Maraj, said.

“We’re not going to let them get away with bullying him and the smear campaigns. It’s not going to work, OK? He has a lot of force behind him and God is protecting him,” she said, ahead of remarks by Trump at the event.

While in 2020 Minaj said she wouldn’t “jump on the Donald Trump bandwagon,” in recent years she’s become a vocal supporter of the president.

Last month, the entertainer made a surprise appearance at Turning Point USA’s annual conference.

“I have the utmost respect and admiration for our president,” Minaj said at the time. 

“He has, I don’t know if he even knows this, but he’s given so many people hope that there’s a chance to beat the bad guys and to win and to do it with your head held high and your integrity intact. He’s from Queens, New York, like me,” she said. 

At Wednesday’s event, the commander in chief expressed his own praise for Minaj, referring to her as the “greatest and most successful female rapper in history” and thanking her for donating “hundreds of thousands of dollars” into the Trump accounts. 

“I just think she’s great,” Trump said of Minaj.

“I didn’t know Nicki and I’ve been hearing over the years she’s a big Trump supporter, or Trump fan. And she took a little heat on occasion because her community isn’t necessarily a Trump fan,” he said.

Trump, who at one point was seen holding hands with Minaj — who was sporting extremely long painted nails — told the audience with a laugh, “I’m going to let my nails grow because I love those nails. I’m going to let those nails grow.”

—Max Rego contributed.

data.title: Democrats exposed as hypocrites on gun rights after fury over Alex Pretti killing
data.description:
In response to the killing of Alex Pretti in Minneapolis over the weekend, there’s been a lot of attention to so-called Republican hypocrisy on the Second Amendment. Pretti was carrying a gun, and was licensed to do so. It’s not clear if he was still armed when he was shot — video evidence strongly suggests the Customs and Border Protection agents disarmed him and then shot him dead. 

In response, several top Republicans have made the wrongful claim that Pretti was carrying a gun in some manner that violated the law: either that he brandished it at officers, or that he didn’t have the right to bring a gun to a protest, or to confront law enforcement while carrying a gun. All of these ideas are wrong, and the individuals who have pushed them — Kash Patel, Scott Bessent, Kristi Noem — are mistaken. 

There’s also this idea that he was breaking the law because he wasn’t carrying ID, which is required for gun owners. Here was Fox News commentator Joey Jones. 

The problem with that is the cops didn’t ask him to show ID. If they had, and if he hadn’t had it on him, they could have charged him a $25 fine. So what? The lack of ID doesn’t have any impact on whether law enforcement was correct to view him as a threat or not. So let’s stop obsessing over that, it’s a non sequitur. 

Instead, let’s robustly defend our principles, and chief among them is the right to bear arms. You can bear arms at protests, and you can bear arms while interacting with law enforcement. And if you happen to bear arms while committing a crime, like resisting arrest or impeding law enforcement, you can’t be killed just because you had a gun on you. 

But let’s not pretend that it’s the Republican officials and solely Republican officials being hypocritical about this. I’ve actually mostly seen criticism of Kash Patel et al from Republicans.  So my question is, where do Democrats actually stand on this?  

As I mentioned earlier this week, the ACLU, for instance, has backpedaled on whether they support concealed carry rights of protesters. I think many progressives do NOT believe that protesters should be armed. They certainly seem to support every restriction on gun ownership and concealed carry that Democratic legislators can think of. 

Sadly, too many politicians in both parties have been willing to compromise on fundamental Second Amendment rights. I’m perfectly willing to admit that many Republicans have been bad on this issue. But most Democrats are significantly worse, and favor all sorts of restrictions on gun ownership, on carrying guns, on which guns you can buy, on how long it takes. Every time anything goes wrong concerning a gun, we are told by Democrats that we need common sense gun control, whatever that is. Are they now saying, in the wake of Pretti’s death, that they no longer support that? 

Please help make it make sense. 

Robby Soave is co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising” and a senior editor for Reason Magazine. This column is an edited transcription of his daily commentary.

data.title: Watch live: Powell gives remarks after Fed holds interest rates steady
data.description:
Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell will give remarks Wednesday afternoon after the nation’s central bank opted to keep short-term interest rates steady between 3.5 and 3.75 percent.

The move comes after the Fed lowered the rate three times during President Trump’s first year back in office amid pressure from the administration. Powell faced criticism from Trump over previous rate pauses and the latest decision marks the first since the Justice Department subpoenaed the Fed as part of a criminal investigation into the chair’s testimony over a $2.5 billion building renovation.

Now, Powell will likely have to shift his messaging to emphasize that rate decisions are driven by economic concerns, not politics.

The event is scheduled to begin at 2:30 p.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Judge temporarily blocks removal of 5-year-old boy, father detained in Minnesota
data.description:
A federal judge has temporarily blocked the removal of a 5-year-old boy and his father detained by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) in Minnesota last week, when the boy was claimed to have been used as “bait” to detain his father.

U.S. Judge Fred Biery’s Monday ruling to Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem, Attorney General Pam Bondi and other officials also ordered that they cannot transfer 5-year-old Liam Conejo Ramos and his father Adrian Conejo Arias “outside of this judicial district during the pendency of this litigation and until further Order of this Court.”

Both are being held at the Dilley Immigration Processing Center in Dilley, Texas.

The father and son came home in Columbia Heights, Minn., from Ramos’s preschool school when masked officers approached them in their driveway, Columbia Heights Public School District Superintendent Zena Stenvik told Minnesota Public Radio last week.

“Another adult living in the home was outside and begged the agents to let him take care of the small child, and was refused,” Stenvik said. “Instead, the agent took the child out of the still-running car, led him to the door and directed him to knock on the door asking to be let in in order to see if anyone else was home, essentially using a 5-year-old as bait.”

DHS claimed that Arias tried to run before he was arrested.

“For the child’s safety, one of our ICE officers remained with the child while the other officers apprehended Conejo Arias,” DHS spokesperson Tricia McLaughlin said in a statement.

She said Arias was from Ecuador and “RELEASED into the U.S. by the Biden administration,” adding that officers “did NOT target a child.”

Stenvik told Minnesota Public Radio that the family had no orders for deportation due to an “active asylum case,” with paperwork that she said she has seen “with my own eyes.”

The arrest led to outrage, with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) saying that his state’s residents want safety, freedom and “what’s best for our kids.”

“Masked agents snatching preschoolers off the street and sending them to Texas detention centers serves none of those purposes,” the governor posted on the social platform X last week. “This campaign of retribution has got to stop.”

Rep. Angie Craig (D-Minn.) also condemned the arrests, calling it the “worst of the damn worst” and accusing ICE of taking Ramos to “use him to lure his father out.”

“This is a time where we should all be outraged,” Craig said in a video posted on X. “If this doesn’t pierce through your humanity, as a Republican member of Congress, if you can’t speak out about this, then you’ve got no humanity left.”

Ramos is one of several children in Columbia Heights detained by ICE this month, including two high school students and a 10-year-old girl. After Ramos and his father were detained, another Ecuadorian man and his 2-year-old daughter in Minnesota were detained by ICE officers.

Irina Vaynerman, an attorney representing Elvis Joel Tipan Echeverria and his daughter, told Minnesota Public Radio that a judge ordered for their release, but “20 minutes later they were put on a flight.”

“Minnesotans are being unlawfully detained and are then being quickly marshalled across state lines by the government,” Vaynerman told the news outlet. “They’re doing that in order to evade the reach of the federal district court in Minnesota.”

data.title: Kristi Noem is skating on thin ice
data.description:
Two innocent civilians are dead and the Bill of Rights is on life support. Renee Good and Alex Pretti were killed in Minneapolis by federal agents. The mother and the intensive care nurse were peaceful protestors exercising their First Amendment constitutional freedoms. They weren’t domestic terrorists.

The dramatic video of Pretti’s killing is outrageous and not for the faint of heart. Border Patrol agents shot him after he courageously tried to protect a woman shoved to the ground by President Trump’s shock troops.

So far, the only casualty in the Trump administration is Border Patrol Commander Greg Bovino, whose ham-handed response to Pretti’s brutal shooting cost him his job. Will his boss, Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem lose her job, too? Inquiring minds want to know.

There’s no mystery in the killings of Good and Pretti. Americans have seen the videotape, ignored the Trump spin about domestic terrorists and rendered judgement. The verdict is, “Enough is enough!”

A national survey conducted for The New York Times by Siena College through Friday, Jan. 23 indicates that Americans want federal immigration enforcement on a short leash. Six in ten voters believe the agency has gone too far. This survey was conducted after Good was killed, but before it could fully measure the impact of the death of Pretti. Things will only get worse for Trump and Noem when the public has time to digest the awful taste of the Pretti killing.

Concern about the extreme actions in Minnesota extends to just about every Democrat, seven of every ten Independents and even one in five Republicans. The most pro-Trump Democratic senator, John Fetterman (D-Pa.), has called for Noem’s resignation. Several congressional Republicans have also seen the videotape and the handwriting on the wall and called for hearings on ICE tactics.

In the midst of the carnage, Trump travelled to Iowa Tuesday to sell his failed economic policies. Trump gained a second term in 2024 with a promise to lower prices on the first day of his second term. But prices continue to rise after he’s been in office again for a full year. Voters want action, not empty promises. His extraordinary efforts to hunt down and deport immigrants only shine a harsh light on his feeble attempts to reduce the cost of living.

The House of Representatives has already passed a budget bill which increases funding for ICE by $10 billion. Now the ball is in the Senate’s court where minority Democrats have some leverage with the 60-vote filibuster rule and only 53 Republican senators. It’s time for the Democratic Senate opposition leader Chuck Schumer of New York and for soft Trump supporters like Sen. Susan Collins (R-Maine) to take a strong stand against tyranny.

Americans may not want to abolish ICE because of their concern about immigration, but they certainly want to rein the agency in. Public disgust over immigration enforcement tactics means that congressional Democrats should use their leverage to demand reforms in the agency’s operations.

Those changes should include reductions in its exorbitant funding, additional training in nonviolent conflict resolution, a ban on masks for officers, and cooperation between federal and state and local authorities investigating immigration agent abuses.

Trump announced Tuesday that he won’t fire Noem. But the ICE Queen is still skating on thin ice. House Democrats on the Homeland Security Committee announced she will be impeached if he doesn’t remove her. Republicans on Capitol Hill already dread the midterm elections and the fallout from the Minnesota carnage will accelerate their party’s descent into political oblivion this November. Trump won’t back down, so it’s up to moderately conservative Republicans to get her replaced.

This descent into fascism is not what the Founders wanted for America. The thing they feared most was that a tyrannical president would use his own personal paramilitary police force to kill Americans who opposed authoritarian rule. This is not what Americans signed up for when they elected Trump to a second term in November 2024. The public will not tolerate this much political chaos much longer.

Brad Bannon is a national Democratic strategist and CEO of Bannon Communications. He writes weekly for The Hill and hosts the popular progressive podcast on power, politics and policy, Deadline D.C. with Brad Bannon.

data.title: NRA pushes back on Trump, Patel saying people who attend protests shouldn't have guns
data.description:
The National Rifle Association (NRA) on Tuesday pushed back against statements from President Trump and FBI Director Kash Patel stating that individuals should not bring guns to protests. 

“The NRA unequivocally believes that all law-abiding citizens have a right to keep and bear arms anywhere they have a legal right to be,” the NRA wrote in a Tuesday post on the social media platform X.

In recent days, Trump and some allies have criticized the protester shot and killed by Border Patrol agents in Minneapolis for having a 9mm hand gun at the site of protest.

Alex Pretti, who was a legal gun owner, was pepper sprayed before he was fatally shot by agents after helping a woman up who was shoved to the ground.

“You can’t have guns. You can’t walk in with guns,” Trump told reporters at the White House on Tuesday.

The president went on to call the incident “very unfortunate.”

On Sunday, Patel said, “You cannot bring a firearm loaded with multiple magazines to any sort of protest that you want. It’s that simple. You don’t have the right to break the law and incite violence,” during an appearance on Fox News’ “Sunday Morning Futures.”

Gun advocates have railed against the Trump administration’s claims, citing Second Amendment rights.

“Peaceful protests while armed isn’t radical—it’s American. The First and Second Amendments protect those rights, and they always have,” Gun Owners of America, a lobbying group, wrote in a Tuesday post on X.

“GOA will hold any administration accountable,” they added.

data.title: Jeffries: Firing Noem wouldn't be enough for Democrats to support DHS funding bill
data.description:
House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) said Wednesday that ousting Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem from her role “is of course not enough” for Democrats to support a funding bill for the agency.

“We have a DHS killing machine,” Jeffries said, referring to two fatal shootings in Minneapolis this month involving immigration officials. “The American people have seen it. This is an administration that promised to deport violent felons. We all support that, but that’s not what’s happening right now.”

On Tuesday, Jeffries and several other House Democrat leaders threatened to launch impeachment proceedings against Noem if President Trump doesn’t fire her over the agency’s ramped up use of force in its immigration enforcement operations. The lawmakers wrote in a joint letter, “We can do this the easy way or the hard way.”

“Donald Trump needs to fire Kristi Noem immediately,” the New York Democrat said Wednesday morning. “We know that she is a malignant, corrupt, pathological liar. She’s unqualified. She never should have been confirmed by Republicans in the United States Senate.”

He also noted that he’s currently in conversation with other House Democrats about the “next steps” in this process. 

Jeffries’ comments follow weeks of unrest in Minnesota, where the Trump administration’s crackdown on illegal immigration has sparked waves of protest across the state. Federal officers shot and killed two American citizens during the operation and shot a Venezuelan migrant in the leg while he attempted to evade arrest. 

DHS has maintained that the officers were acting in self-defense. Elected Democrats and state officials have vehemently disputed these claims and criticized the administration for making public assessments prior to conclusive investigative results.

Following the Jan. 7 killing of Renee Good by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement officer and the death of 37-year-old intensive care nurse Alex Pretti by Border Patrol agents over the weekend, many Senate Democrats expressed outrage and stated they will not back a six-bill funding package to avoid a partial government shutdown at the end of the week.

The House passed the minibus last week despite the opposition. The Republican-backed measure needs a minimum of eight Democrats in the upper chamber in order to advance.

Sen. John Fetterman (D-Penn.) is the only Senate Democrat so far to say that he will join Republicans and vote in favor of the funding bills. Fetterman, who has joined Democrats’ calls for Noem’s firing and criticized federal agent’s tactics in the Minnesota operation, said on Monday that he “will never vote to shut our government down.”

In a statement following Pretti’s shooting, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) said he and other Democrats in the legislative chamber will not vote for the package unless allocated funding for DHS is stripped. The DHS bill would give $10 billion more to ICE. 

Removing funding for the agency would require calling the House back into session to re-vote on the package — something Republican leadership says they are unwilling to do. 

“What’s happening in Minnesota is appalling — and unacceptable in any American city,” Schumer said in a statement Saturday. “Democrats sought common sense reforms in the Department of Homeland Security spending bill, the DHS bill is woefully inadequate to rein in the abuses of ICE. I will vote no.”

Jeffries further criticized Noem’s leadership Wednesday, saying, “She is creating harm to the American people.”

“Taxpayer dollars are actually being used by the department to kill American citizens, to brutalize law-abiding immigrant families,” he said. “That’s unacceptable, so she’s gotta go.”

data.title: Homan says meetings with Walz, Frey a 'productive starting point'
data.description:
White House border czar Tom Homan said recent meetings with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) have gone well in the wake of recent tension over President Trump’s immigration agenda in the state.

“Today I met with Governor Walz, Mayor Frey, and top law enforcement officials to discuss the issues on the ground in Minnesota. We all agree that we need to support our law enforcement officers and get criminals off the streets,” Homan said in a post on the social platform X on Tuesday.

“While we don’t agree on everything, these meetings were a productive starting point and I look forward to more conversations with key stakeholders in the days ahead,” he added.

Homan’s comments follow weeks of tension in Minnesota over Trump’s immigration agenda. The recent fatal shootings of Alex Pretti and Renee Good in Minneapolis by federal immigration authorities have sparked widespread backlash toward the administration.

Frey said that he and Minneapolis Police Department Chief Brian O’Hara “met with Border Czar Homan and had a productive conversation.”

“I reiterated that my main ask is for Operation Metro Surge to end as quickly as possible. Public safety works best when it’s built on community trust, not tactics that create fear or division,” he added in a Tuesday post on X.

Trump on Monday announced that Homan was headed to Minnesota following Saturday’s fatal shooting of Pretti.

“I am sending Tom Homan to Minnesota tonight. He has not been involved in that area, but knows and likes many of the people there,” Trump said on his Truth Social platform. “Tom is tough but fair and will report to me.”

Walz’s office on Tuesday released a statement on the meeting to NewsNation.

“Governor Walz met with Tom Homan this morning and reiterated Minnesota’s priorities: impartial investigations into the Minneapolis shootings involving federal agents, a swift, significant reduction in the number of federal forces in Minnesota, and an end to the campaign of retribution against Minnesota,” Walz’s office said in the statement.

data.title: Live updates Senate tackles funding package amid DHS furor; Trump touts 'Trump accounts'
data.description:
The Senate on Wednesday is working to advance a six-bill spending package, despite calls from Democrats to separate out Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding after the deadly weekend shooting of a U.S. citizen in Minneapolis by federal immigration officials.

The incident has put Republicans on defense, and they are starting to splinter over support for DHS Secretary Kristi Noem. She retains the confidence of President Trump, however.

Overnight, Minnesota Rep. Ilhan Omar (D), who represents Minneapolis and some of its suburbs, was attacked at town hall in the city, as she called for Noem’s impeachment.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio, meanwhile, is testifying before the Senate on the administration’s actions in Venezuela. Watch live here. Trump, earlier Wednesday, spoke at a Treasury Department summit, where he promoted his investment accounts for newborn babies.

Later, the Federal Reserve will announce its interest rate decision and chair Jerome Powell will deliver remarks. The central bank is expected to keep rates steady.

Follow live below.

data.title: More 'No Kings' protests planned for spring in wake of Minnesota deaths
data.description:
Organizers behind the massive “No Kings” demonstrations that swept across the U.S. last year are planning another round of rallies this spring as furor grows over the fatal shootings of two U.S. citizens by federal law enforcement officers.

The next day of protests is scheduled for March 28, with a flagship event in the Twin Cities, the center of the Trump administration’s latest immigration crackdown.

“In 2025, millions of Americans came together in nonviolent protest to oppose the growing authoritarian actions of the Trump administration and affirm that this nation belongs to its people, not to kings,” a message on the “No Kings” website stated.

“Since then, people have continued to rise up nonviolently against the Trump administration’s ongoing brutality and abuses of power, including the latest escalation in Minnesota,” it continued.

Ezra Levin, the co-executive director of the nonprofit Invisible, told The Associated Press that organizers expect it to be “the largest protest in American history,” predicting that as many as 9 million people would participate.

Levin told the outlet that plans for another round of protests had already been in progress, but the deaths of Alex Pretti and Renee Good this month prompted them to shift focus toward Minneapolis.

Pretti, an ICU nurse, was shot and killed on Saturday during what the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) called an “armed struggle” with U.S. Border Patrol agents.

Federal authorities said Pretti had approached agents with a gun, but video evidence disputed that narrative, showing Pretti holding a cellphone in his hand when the incident began. It also appeared to show Pretti’s firearm, which he was legally permitted to carry, being removed from his waist by one agent before another fires shots.

The shooting inflamed tensions in a city already on edge following the death of Good, who was fatally shot in her SUV by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer Jonathan Ross.

Senior Trump administration officials were quick to cast both shootings as self-defense, while also blaming Democratic city and state leaders for refusing to cooperate with federal authorities.

Trump is now facing more pushback on Capitol Hill, where both Republicans and Democrats have raised concerns about the administration’s immigration enforcement tactics and called for a thorough investigation.

Bipartisan calls for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to resign have also been mounting in the wake of Pretti’s death.

Trump pulled some Border Patrol agents out of the state this week and sent border czar Tom Homan to replace Commander-at-Large Greg Bovino on the ground, a sign the White House may be softening its stance amid the political fallout.
